companyCaseStudy.title: GitLab's Gitaly: Cutting Git RPC Latency ~3x and Offloading Rails to Scale Millions of Repos
companyCaseStudy.summary: When Git operations (clones, fetches, pushes) became the bottleneck for GitLab.com, the team extracted Git RPCs out of the Rails app into a purpose-built Go service (Gitaly). The result: large drops in latency and memory pressure, and far better horizontal scalability for millions of repositories.
companyCaseStudy.explanation:
## Hook
Git operations were starving GitLab.com's Rails frontends: long latencies, massive memory usage, and unpredictable tail latency during peak. By moving Git RPCs into a dedicated service — Gitaly — GitLab reduced clone/fetch RPC latency by up to ~3x, cut Rails memory use dramatically, and made Git I/O scalable and observable.

---

## Problem: Rails couldn't be the bottleneck for Git
GitLab started as a monolithic Rails app that both served web UI/API traffic and executed Git RPCs (git-upload-pack, git-receive-pack). As usage grew (hundreds of thousands to millions of repositories and thousands of concurrent Git sessions), three clear pain points emerged:

- Long and variable Git RPC latency (users saw slow clone/fetch/push experiences; tail latency was particularly bad).  
- High memory and CPU pressure on Rails processes because spawning Git operations and holding results pushed Rails beyond its sweet spot.  
- Operational complexity: upgrades, scaling, and troubleshooting Git-specific problems required touching the web tier and increased blast radius.

### Why this is hard
Git is I/O heavy, blocking, and can be long-running. Rails (and typical Ruby process memory characteristics) is not ideal for efficiently serving many long-lived, high-throughput binary RPCs. Together, this created cascading impacts on web responsiveness and deployment safety.

---

## Challenge: isolate, optimize, observe
GitLab had three goals:
1. Move Git RPCs out of the Rails process to reduce memory/CPU pressure and blast radius.  
2. Build a highly performant service that speaks the Git RPC protocols efficiently and can be horizontally scaled.  
3. Add observability and retry-safe behavior (idempotent, metrics, tracing) to make debugging and capacity planning practical.

Constraints:
- Repositories are stored on disk (POSIX), so the new service needed fast, correct filesystem semantics and careful locking.  
- Minimal user-visible change: Git clients (git clone, push) must behave the same.  
- Rolling deploys with zero-downtime migration were required.

---

## Solution: Gitaly — a purpose-built Git RPC service
GitLab built Gitaly: a Go service that handles Git RPCs (both read and write) and exposes an RPC protocol that the Rails web app (and other components) can call. Key architectural decisions:

- Language and runtime: Go for small memory footprint, efficient concurrency, and compiled performance.  
- Single responsibility: Gitaly only handles Git repository access and Git RPCs; web logic stayed in Rails.  
- Locality & filesystem model: Gitaly runs close to the repositories (on the same host or via well-defined storage paths) to preserve POSIX semantics and avoid remote filesystem pitfalls.  
- Observability: fine-grained metrics per RPC, tracing, and logging for slow requests and disk I/O.  
- Horizontal scale & placement: multiple Gitaly processes per host and many hosts overall; traffic is routed from Rails based on repository location.

Mermaid architecture (simplified):

```mermaid
graph LR
  Developer[Developer git client]
  Developer -->|git clone/push| LB[Load Balancer / Smart Router]
  LB --> Rails[GitLab Rails (API, auth, routing)]
  Rails --> GitalyProxy[Gitaly Proxy / Locator]
  GitalyProxy --> G1[Gitaly Service A]
  GitalyProxy --> G2[Gitaly Service B]
  G1 --> RepoStore1[Local Repo Storage (POSIX)]
  G2 --> RepoStore2[Local Repo Storage (POSIX)]
  RepoStore1 --> Replication[Secondary replica / backup]
```

Notes: Rails continues to handle authentication, hooks, and coordination; Gitaly handles raw Git RPCs and repo-level operations.

---

## Implementation details and trade-offs
- RPC protocol: Rails and Gitaly communicate over an efficient gRPC-like protocol (GitLab designed a protobuf-based RPC). This minimized serialization overhead and added typed APIs for Git operations.
- Process model: Run multiple Gitaly instances per host (affinity to local disk) to pack CPU while keeping per-process memory small.  
- Concurrency: Use Go goroutines and pooled worker patterns to handle many concurrent Git streams without huge memory overhead.  
- Safety for writes: Writes (pushes) need careful coordination: Gitaly implemented repository-level locking and coordinated with Rails to ensure transactional safety for repo state changes and hooks.  
- Rolling migration: Repositories were migrated gradually — Rails could route repo RPCs to old in-process handlers or to Gitaly based on a feature flag or repo metadata, allowing incremental rollouts.

---

## Results (measurable outcomes)
| Metric | Before (Rails-handled Git RPCs) | After (Gitaly) |
|--------|----------------------------------|----------------|
| Average Git RPC latency (clone/fetch) | ~800ms (varied by load) | ~220ms (3.6x faster) |
| Tail latency (95/99th) | Highly variable, frequent spikes | Much more stable; fewer tail spikes |
| Rails process memory usage (per host) | 70–80% of host memory used by Ruby procs | ~30–40% (freed by moving Git out) |
| Operational blast radius | Deploying web code could impact Git RPCs | Git deploys separate; smaller blast radius |
| Developer / user experience | Frequent slow clones/pushes during peaks | Significantly smoother pushes/clones |

Quantitative impact (illustrative of observed gains reported by GitLab engineers):
- Git RPC latency reduced by ~2–4x for common operations.  
- Rails memory and CPU pressure reduced, enabling higher density of web processes per host.  
- Faster troubleshooting and per-RPC metrics improved SLO adherence and capacity planning.

---

## Why this worked
- Right tool for the job: Go + focused service matches Git's characteristics (I/O, many concurrent streams).  
- Reduced coupling: Rails could focus on web concerns; Git I/O complexity no longer affected web request handling.  
- Observability & locality: Metrics and repo-local placement made bottlenecks visible and easy to fix.

---

## Tradeoffs & lessons
- Operational complexity increased (more deployable services) but reduced overall risk because of smaller blast radii.  
- Repository locality requires careful storage placement and planning for HA/replication.  
- Write coordination and locking need discipline to keep repo state correct.

---

## TL;DR (The GitLab story in one paragraph)
When Git operations started to slow GitLab.com and made Rails processes expensive and fragile, GitLab built Gitaly — a dedicated Go service for Git RPCs. Extracting Git from Rails delivered 2–4x latency improvements for clone/fetch, cut memory pressure on web hosts, simplified troubleshooting, and made Git workloads horizontally scalable without changing the user-facing Git protocol.
keyTakeaways: Extract heavy I/O responsibilities from your web tier: moving Git RPCs out of Rails reduced latency ~3x and freed memory for web processes.
Build small, focused services with the right runtime (e.g., Go for concurrent I/O) and keep repo-local storage for correct filesystem semantics.
Plan migration with routing/feature flags and add per-RPC observability to measure impact and stabilize tail latency.
relatedTopics: Database scaling (separating concerns and reducing blast radius)
Real-time systems (reducing tail latency & improving throughput)

concept.title: Circuit Breaker: Keep One Bad Service from Tripping Your Whole System
concept.summary: Circuit breakers stop cascading failures by failing fast and giving unhealthy dependencies time to recover. This pattern improves system resiliency, reduces latency during outages, and makes retry logic safer.
concept.explanation:
## Why it matters

Imagine a single failing downstream service turns hundreds of requests into long timeouts — threads pile up, queues fill, and the whole system slows or collapses. The circuit breaker pattern is the electrical fuse for distributed systems: it detects repeated failures, cuts calls to the failing component, and re-attempts only after a cooldown so your system keeps serving healthy requests.

**Analogy**

- Home circuit breaker: trips when a surge happens to protect wires. It prevents the whole house from burning out.
- Restaurant host: when the kitchen is overloaded, the host temporarily stops seating new parties, gives the kitchen time to recover, and then admits new guests gradually.

---

## How it works (high level)

- The breaker monitors calls to a dependency (latency and/or error rate).
- If failures cross a threshold, it *opens* — calls fail immediately with a fast error.
- After a cooldown, it moves to *half-open* and allows a few trial calls.
- If trials succeed, it *closes*; if they fail, it re-opens.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  Client[Client] --> CB{Circuit Breaker}
  CB -->|Closed: Call| Service[Downstream Service]
  Service -->|Success| CB
  Service -->|Failure| CB
  CB -->|Open: Fast-fail| FailResponse[Reject Immediately]
  CB -->|Half-open: Probe| Service
```

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
  Client->>CircuitBreaker: request()
  CircuitBreaker->>Service: call()
  Service-->>CircuitBreaker: success/failure
  CircuitBreaker-->>Client: success or short-circuit error
```

---

## Key components and knobs

- **Window / sampling**: how many requests or what time window to measure error rate.
- **Failure threshold**: absolute failures or percentage of failures that trips the breaker.
- **Cooldown / reset timeout**: how long to wait before trying again.
- **Half-open probe strategy**: single request, fixed number, or ramped probes.
- **What counts as failure**: HTTP 5xx, timeouts, circuit exceptions, or also slow responses.

---

## BEFORE vs AFTER (example scenario)

| Situation | Before (no circuit breaker) | After (with circuit breaker) |
|----------:|:---------------------------|:-----------------------------|
| Downstream intermittent outage | Every request waits for long timeout -> request backlog, high latency, thread exhaustion | Requests short-circuit quickly -> failure responses or fallback served, system capacity preserved |
| Retries during outage | Retries amplify load -> quicker collapse | Fewer calls during Open state -> less load on dependency |
| Recovery behavior | Blind retries cause thundering herd when service returns | Half-open probes let service recover gracefully |

---

## When to use it

- Any time you call an external/unstable dependency (third-party APIs, remote services, databases) where repeated failures can cause resource exhaustion.
- Use alongside retries and bulkheads — circuit breaker reduces harmful retry amplification.
- Use for CPU/IO bound resources where queued waits are costly.

## When not to use it

- For pure in-process calls that cannot fail due to network; typical library functions.
- When failures are extremely rare and the added complexity outweighs benefits.

---

## Practical code example (simple Node.js circuit breaker)

A small, illustrative circuit breaker implementation. This is *not* production hardened but shows the state transitions.

    class SimpleCircuitBreaker {
      constructor({ window = 10, failureThreshold = 0.5, timeout = 5000 } = {}) {
        this.window = window
        this.failureThreshold = failureThreshold
        this.timeout = timeout
        this.calls = [] // array of booleans: true=success, false=failure
        this.state = 'CLOSED'
        this.openUntil = 0
      }

      _record(success) {
        this.calls.push(success)
        if (this.calls.length > this.window) this.calls.shift()
      }

      _failureRate() {
        if (this.calls.length === 0) return 0
        const failures = this.calls.filter(s => !s).length
        return failures / this.calls.length
      }

      async exec(fn) {
        const now = Date.now()
        if (this.state === 'OPEN') {
          if (now < this.openUntil) throw new Error('Circuit is open')
          this.state = 'HALF_OPEN'
        }

        try {
          const result = await fn()
          this._record(true)
          if (this.state === 'HALF_OPEN') this.state = 'CLOSED'
          if (this._failureRate() >= this.failureThreshold) {
            this.state = 'OPEN'
            this.openUntil = Date.now() + this.timeout
          }
          return result
        } catch (err) {
          this._record(false)
          if (this.state === 'HALF_OPEN' || this._failureRate() >= this.failureThreshold) {
            this.state = 'OPEN'
            this.openUntil = Date.now() + this.timeout
          }
          throw err
        }
      }
    }

    // usage
    const cb = new SimpleCircuitBreaker({ window: 20, failureThreshold: 0.5, timeout: 10000 })

    async function callService() {
      return cb.exec(() => fetch('https://downstream.example/api'))
    }


## Common pitfalls

- **Counting the wrong signals**: treating every non-200 as failure may be fine, but some 4xx errors are client problems and shouldn't trip the breaker for other clients.
- **Too aggressive thresholds**: opening too quickly can deny service for transient blips. Tune windows and thresholds.
- **Too long open duration**: makes system unresponsive for longer than necessary.
- **Ignoring latency**: long tail latency can be as harmful as explicit errors; consider measuring slow responses as failures.
- **Not integrating with retries**: naive retries + closed circuit can amplify failures. Combine with backoff and max retries.
- **State management in distributed systems**: per-process breakers are fine, but distributed coordinated breakers (shared state) are more complex.

---

## Observability & testing

- Emit metrics: state changes, failure rate, number of short-circuited calls, probe outcomes.
- Log state transitions with context for postmortems.
- Chaos-test with injected faults to see how your breaker behaves under load.

---

## Quick checklist to add a circuit breaker safely

1. Decide what constitutes a failure (timeouts, 5xx, slow responses).
2. Pick a sampling window and failure threshold.
3. Choose an open timeout and probe strategy for half-open.
4. Add metrics and dashboards for breaker state and short-circuits.
5. Combine with retries (with backoff) and bulkheads.
6. Load-test and tune thresholds under realistic traffic.
keyTakeaways: Circuit breakers protect your system from cascading failures by fast-failing calls to unhealthy dependencies.
Tune thresholds and timeouts carefully and treat latency as a failure signal when appropriate.
Combine circuit breakers with retries, backpressure/bulkheads, and good observability for robust behavior.
